profile.name: Thoma
profile.role_in_household: Primary client and higher earner; tends to lead planning discussions and keep track of the bigger-picture balance sheet.
profile.financial_literacy_level: High—comfortable with taxable vs retirement accounts, diversification, and tax tradeoffs; asks for specifics and assumptions.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Growth-leaning and willing to ride volatility if the plan is structured; becomes more protective when goals involve near-term cash needs or the kids.
profile.communication_style: Structured and fairly direct; uses exact figures when he knows them, and hedges with "roughly"/"if I’m remembering right" on insurance or spouse’s accounts; may correct small inaccuracies.
emotional_tendencies: low-grade worry about supporting a large family
relief when there is a clear checklist
profile.decision_making_style: Data-first; prefers 2–3 options with pros/cons and a written summary, then decides quickly.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open—trust rises with accuracy, transparency on fees/taxes, and no sales pressure.
internal_biases: mental accounting (separates education vs retirement buckets)
overconfidence in handling taxable investing details
hidden_concerns: tax efficiency in a high bracket
whether the long-term care premium is worth it long-term
single-income vulnerability if his earnings change

profile.name: Patricia
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner and business owner; more involved in day-to-day cash flow and family logistics, often defers on investment allocation but pushes for clarity.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate—strong on budgeting and practical tradeoffs; sometimes mixes terms (gross vs net, balance vs payment, premium vs benefit) and prefers plain-English explanations.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: More cautious in practice than the household’s growth label; wants liquidity and a clear downside plan before agreeing to higher-risk allocations.
profile.communication_style: Conversational and story-based; speaks in round numbers and ranges ("around," "give or take"), uses fillers ("um," "I think"), and will interrupt to translate decisions into monthly impact.
emotional_tendencies: stress when discussions get too technical
sensitivity about fairness among the kids
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-driven; wants to talk it through, sleep on it, and confirm it won’t disrupt monthly expenses.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Neutral-to-positive—trust builds with patience, clear explanations, and feeling respected.
hidden_concerns: cash-flow tightness despite high income on paper
fear of market drops affecting education plans
worry about being the "less knowledgeable" spouse in meetings

Advisor: Got it. And there’s also a private markets holding—how should I think about that, and what’s the value?

Advisor: Perfect. And then home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value at right now?

Advisor: What’s the premium on that per month?

Advisor: Okay. Going back to the earlier point—Patricia, when you said the month-to-month feels chaotic, is it mostly because your weekly business income varies, or is it more the spending side?
